EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific and Gulf moisture arriving from the southwest rode up and over shallow arctic air at the surface across the eastern Carolinas on January 25, 2026, leading to a thin glaze of freezing rain and some sleet. The largest accumulations of freezing rain, up to 0.35 in thickness, was reported from Lumberton and Red Springs in Robeson County, NC, as well as Florence, SC. Lesser amounts of ice were reported from most other locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KFLO ASOS measured 0.35 of ice during the storm. Several car accidents occurred, including an overturned truck in Quinby, SC, but no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With strong cold air damming in place, areas of light drizzle developed in the presence of surface temperatures at or just below freezing in portions of southeast South Carolina. The cold air was quite shallow, providing an ideal temperature profile for freezing rain and freezing drizzle. The event occurred in the early morning hours before surface temperatures warmed above freezing and the drizzle came to an end. Only light ice amounts were observed, with only limited impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain and freezing drizzle produced a light glaze of ice, mostly on elevated and exposed surfaces across much of the county. Ice did not impact roadways or other infrastructure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted much of southeast South Carolina with significant snow accumulations and very cold wind chill temperatures. An area of low pressure shifted off the Southeast coast while deepening, ushering in a very cold air mass across the region. The depth of cold air was sufficient to support snow as the primary precipitation type. Rain quickly transitioned to snow during the afternoon hours, which continued into the evening before ending. Most of the snow accumulation impacted areas along and north of Interstate 16, with the highest amounts reported across Screven, Bulloch, and Effingham counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local criteria for an Extreme Cold Warning is temperature or wind chill of 10 degrees or less. The wind chill temperature first fell below 10 degrees at the Beaufort Marine Corps Air Station (KNBC) at 21:45 EST, the Beaufort WeatherSTEM site at 23:00 EST, and the Beaufort Weatherflow site at 23:11 EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed across the South Carolina Piedmont during the early morning of the 25th. The precipitation began as mostly sleet before mixing with, and eventually changing to freezing rain later in the morning. The precipitation finally tapered off during the evening. Total sleet accumulations of 0.25 to 0.5 inches occurred, with around 0.25 inch of ice accreting on top of the sleet. This made for very hazardous driving conditions. Due to unusually cold weather following the event, a couple of days of refreezing resulted in travel difficulties continuing during the overnight and morning hours.

A winter storm impacted much of southeast South Carolina with significant snow accumulations and very cold wind chill temperatures. An area of low pressure shifted off the Southeast coast while deepening, ushering in a very cold air mass across the region. The depth of cold air was sufficient to support snow as the primary precipitation type. Rain quickly transitioned to snow during the afternoon hours, which continued into the evening before ending. Most of the snow accumulation impacted areas along and north of Interstate 16, with the highest amounts reported across Screven, Bulloch, and Effingham counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of snow impacted the inland portion of Berkeley County and produced widespread accumulations of 2 to 3 inches. The highest reported totals were 3 inches around Moncks Corner, 2.9 inches in Sangaree, 2.2 inches near College Park, 2 inches in Bonneau, and 2 inches in the Goose Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted much of southeast South Carolina with significant snow accumulations and very cold wind chill temperatures. An area of low pressure shifted off the Southeast coast while deepening, ushering in a very cold air mass across the region. The depth of cold air was sufficient to support snow as the primary precipitation type. Rain quickly transitioned to snow during the afternoon hours, which continued into the evening before ending. Most of the snow accumulation impacted areas along and north of Interstate 16, with the highest amounts reported across Screven, Bulloch, and Effingham counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow impacted the inland half of Colleton County. Snowfall accumulations generally ranged from a dusting up to 1.5 inches. The highest reported amounts were in the Walterboro area of 1 to 1.5 inches. However, Colleton County Emergency Management reported almost 40 snow and ice covered roads across the county which made travel quite treacherous. Impacted roads include Highway 17A from the Dorchester County line all the way to Walterboro, Sidneys Road and Bullseye Lane, Hampton Street, and mile marker 68 on Interstate 95 northbound. Unfortunately, the dangerous travel conditions did result in a fatal car accident in the 9800 block of Sidneys Road near Bullseye Lane. One person died and another was seriously injured in the accident at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a week, a winter storm impacted the South Carolina mountains as an area of low pressure intensified across the Deep South. In this case, all precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling during the late evening of the 30th and continued off and on for around 24 hours, with periods of heavy snow becoming common during the morning of the 31st. By the time the snow tapered off, 5 to 8 inch totals along with locally higher amounts were reported across the area.

For the second time in a week, a winter storm impacted the Piedmont of upstate South Carolina as an area of low pressure intensified across the Deep South. In this case, after a brief period of rain, or a mix of rain and snow at the outset, the bulk of the precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling during the pre-dawn hours of the 31st and continued through much of the day. By the time the snow tapered off during the afternoon, 3 to 5 inch totals were reported across much of the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on January 31 and ended on February 1, ranging from 7 to 12 inches across the county, with the highest totals across southeast parts of the county. Government offices were closed the day following the event, with county schools switching to remote learning for two days. Impacts from the storm were limited.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low offshore and upper trough combined with deep arctic air led to significant snowfall across the Carolinas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on January 31 and ended on February 1, ranging from 3 to 8 inches across the zone, with the highest values near the state line. Government offices were closed and schools switched to remote learning for the day following the storm. Strong winds contributed to power outages across the Grand Strand, with almost 500 people without power Sunday morning. Impacts from the storm were limited.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on January 30 and ended on February 1, ranging from 6 to 8 inches across the county. Government offices were closed the day after the event, with schools switching to remote learning for two days. Impacts from the storm were limited.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With strong cold air damming in place, areas of light drizzle developed in the presence of surface temperatures at or just below freezing in portions of southeast South Carolina. The cold air was quite shallow, providing an ideal temperature profile for freezing rain and freezing drizzle. The event occurred in the early morning hours before surface temperatures warmed above freezing and the drizzle came to an end. Only light ice amounts were observed, with only limited impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain and freezing drizzle produced a light glaze of ice, mostly on elevated and exposed surfaces across the county. The light glaze of ice did produce a slippery stretch of roadway on Interstate 26 between mile marker 181 and 182 where an overturned vehicle was reported. There was one report of a few hundredths of an inch of ice accumulation on leaves in the Reevesville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unique snow setup for South Carolina developed as a strong coastal low strengthening offshore, while a upper level disturbance shifted over our area. This provided sufficient synoptic and mesoscale lift for widespread heavy snow across SC along with unusually high snow to liquid ratios between 15-20 to 1. A brief period of sleet-graupel occurred at the onset of the event following by 12 hours of light-moderate snow across the area, with highest amounts in the Pee Dee region of SC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple measurements across the county of between 2 and 5.3 inches of storm total snowfall. Highest measured amount in the county was 5.3 inches near Bishopville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple measurements across the county from COOP, CoCoRAHS, public, and spotters of between 2 to 5 inches of storm total snowfall. Highest measured amount in the county was 5 inches near Lugoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic ice storm setup developed across the Midlands of South Carolina January 25th. Cold air damming at the surface and strong overrunning aloft produced light and moderate freezing across the Central Midlands with sleet predominant in the Northern Midlands. The southern Midlands saw a shorter period of freezing rain and rain. Damage was primarily due to tree and limb damage along with associated hazardous and impassable roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and powerline damage near Cheraw resulting in roughly 1000 power outages due to storm damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted much of southeast South Carolina with significant snow accumulations and very cold wind chill temperatures. An area of low pressure shifted off the Southeast coast while deepening, ushering in a very cold air mass across the region. The depth of cold air was sufficient to support snow as the primary precipitation type. Rain quickly transitioned to snow during the afternoon hours, which continued into the evening before ending. Most of the snow accumulation impacted areas along and north of Interstate 16, with the highest amounts reported across Screven, Bulloch, and Effingham counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow produced accumulations of 2 to 4 inches across the inland portion of Jasper County. The highest reported amounts include 4 inches in the Pineland area and 2.5 inches in Ridgeland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a week, a winter storm impacted the South Carolina Piedmont  as an area of low pressure intensified across the Deep South. In this case, all precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling during the early morning of the 31st and continued off and on for almost 24 hours, with periods of heavy snow becoming common during the afternoon. By the time the snow tapered off, 6 to 10 inch totals were reported across much of the area.

For the second time in a week, a winter storm impacted the South Carolina Piedmont  as an area of low pressure intensified across the Deep South. In this case, all precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling during the early morning of the 31st and continued off and on for almost 24 hours, with periods of heavy snow becoming common during the afternoon. By the time the snow tapered off, 6 to 10 inch totals were reported across much of the area.

Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed in areas along and near the Blue Ridge escarpment during the late evening of the 24th, beginning mainly as sleet. The sleet, occasionally mixed with snow continued into the early morning of the 25th, when it began to mix with and eventually change to freezing rain. The freezing rain was mostly light until a band of heavy freezing rain moved over the area late in the afternoon. Total sleet accumulations of 1 to 2 inches occurred, with around 0.5 inch (locally as high as 0.75 inch) ice accretion on top of the sleet, resulting in very hazardous driving conditions. Unusually cold temperatures following the event resulted in travel difficulties that lingered for several days.

Precipitation associated with an intensifying low pressure system over the Deep South developed across the eastern South Carolina Piedmont during the early morning of the 25th. The precipitation began as mostly sleet before mixing with, and eventually changing to freezing rain later in the morning. The precipitation finally tapered off during the evening. Total sleet accumulations of around an inch occurred, with 0.10 to 0.25 inch of ice accreting on top of the sleet. This made for very hazardous driving conditions. Due to unusually cold weather following the event, a couple of days of refreezing resulted in travel difficulties continuing during the overnight and morning hours.

A unique snow setup for South Carolina developed as a strong coastal low strengthening offshore, while a upper level disturbance shifted over our area. This provided sufficient synoptic and mesoscale lift for widespread heavy snow across SC along with unusually high snow to liquid ratios between 15-20 to 1. A brief period of sleet-graupel occurred at the onset of the event following by 12 hours of light-moderate snow across the area, with highest amounts in the Pee Dee region of SC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer near McCormick measured 3.5 inches of snow. CoCoRAHS near McCormick  measured 3.2 inches of snow.
